role_summary: Co-founder of SNCC; trained by James Lawson at the 1958-1960 Nashville workshops; one of the original 13 Freedom Riders (1961); SNCC director for the Selma Project (1962-1965), the organizing groundwork that preceded the 1965 Selma to Montgomery marches; Distinguished Senior Scholar in Residence at the Candler School of Theology, Emory University.

photo.alt: Bernard LaFayette, U.S. Congress office portrait, 2020
photo.photographer: Office of Representative Terri Sewell, United States Congress

ai_reading: LaFayette is referenced three times across the catalog, in the SNCC-Nashville-organizers cohort and in the broader Selma backgrounding. He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page anchors the Nashville-workshop-trainee cohort the James Lawson catalog page identifies as the catalog's most-influential not-itself-named figure cluster: LaFayette, Diane Nash, John Lewis, James Bevel, Marion Barry, and C. T. Vivian all trained at Lawson's Tuesday-night Clark Memorial workshops. The novel observation his page surfaces is the field-organizer-vs-front-line-protester distinction: LaFayette's 1962-1965 Selma groundwork (voter registration, citizenship schools, the structural conditions that made the 1965 march possible) is the catalog's clearest example of pre-event organizing erased by the photographed-event historiography, the 1965 Bloody Sunday and Selma to Montgomery march absorbing most of the cultural attention while LaFayette's three-year on-the-ground preparation receives less.
biographical_paragraph: LaFayette was born July 29, 1940 in Tampa, Florida. He enrolled at American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville in 1958 and attended James Lawson's Tuesday-night nonviolence workshops at Clark Memorial Methodist Church across 1959-1960, training alongside John Lewis, Diane Nash, James Bevel, Bernard Lee, Marion Barry, and others who would form SNCC at the April 1960 Shaw University conference. He was one of the original thirteen Freedom Riders who left Washington, D.C. on May 4, 1961; the Riders were attacked in Rock Hill, South Carolina, in Anniston and Birmingham, Alabama, and again in Montgomery. Between 1962 and 1965 he served as SNCC's project director for Selma, Alabama, building voter registration and citizenship schools across Dallas County in the years before the 1965 Selma to Montgomery campaign drew national attention; the field-organizing groundwork his project laid is the structural condition that made the photographed 1965 events possible. He took a Bachelor's in philosophy and political science from American Baptist (1961), a Master's in education (1972) and Doctorate in education (1974) from Harvard. He has served as president of the American Baptist Theological Seminary, dean of education at the Center for Nonviolence and Peace Studies at the University of Rhode Island, and Distinguished Senior Scholar in Residence at the Candler School of Theology at Emory University. His 2013 memoir In Peace and Freedom: My Journey in Selma (with Kathryn Lee Johnson, University Press of Kentucky) is the canonical scholarly source for his 1962-1965 Selma chapter.

sources.title: Bernard LaFayette Jr. with Kathryn Lee Johnson, In Peace and Freedom: My Journey in Selma
sources.publisher: University Press of Kentucky, 2013; WorldCat OCLC 828423064
